text: last night, after his apology, it was wrong coming in without his permission … But what could he possibly have that he didn’t want her to see, anyway? It wasn’t like bots kept things for sentimental value. Hell, most humans didn’t even do that. Lara was an exception. People only cared about things relating to survival — food and drink, warmth and shelter. Though the specific needs were different, bots operated the same way . A large chest sat at the foot of the bed, with several tools, brushes, and narrow rods laid atop it. Amidst these items was the gun he’d held when he burst into the toilet room last night, having rushed to protect Lara after hearing her scream . Lara stared at the dull black gun. Her heart thumped. If she’d had one that night … She stepped forward and reached for it, stopping suddenly just before her fingers touched the metal. Ronin hadn’t been stopped by three shots to his abdomen; a single cut could be the end for a human. This little gun wouldn’t do any damage to Ronin . Why did he own it? Why had he left it behind ? A test, perhaps. But if she fired it at him — assuming she even hit her target — it wouldn’t do enough harm for her to escape retaliation . She swallowed her temptation and dropped her hand, moving on to the double doors on the opposite wall. They slid open on hidden rails. It was a little room, even smaller than her shack, with clothing hung on either side. Ronin’s clothing. Leaning in, she took a deep breath, realizing what she’d done only after Ronin’s earth-and-steel scent filled her nostrils . Wanderer. Dustwalker. How much time had he spent out there ? Why was she thinking about him so much ? Annoyed with herself, she returned to her room and closed the door behind her. Without meaning to, she compared it to Ronin’s . She made the bed, keeping the memory of his at the front of her mind. No matter how many ways she tried, no matter how careful she was, the blanket always hung off one side more than the other, always had unsightly lumps and wrinkles in it . It was going to get messed up when she went to sleep. Why bother ? With a huff, she sat on the edge of the bed, drumming her fingers atop the soft blanket. Her gaze wandered, eventually stopping on the window. Afternoon light filtered through the coverings, filling the room with warmth, and the shadows of the trees danced on the bottom panes . There were three rows of windows outside, she realized. This was only the second floor. She’d seen a third last night, staring up at Ronin’s dark, looming home. It had to be directly above her room . So, where was the staircase up to the next level? That seemed a large thing to miss, having been through all the rooms. There was another sliding door on one of her walls, leading to a recess barely large enough for a child to lie in; a smaller version of the room Ronin kept his clothes in . “Enough with the damned clothes, already,” she muttered . Her search of the room was quick, but thorough, ending when she wrestled the heavy dresser forward to reveal the solid wall behind it . Lara entered the hallway, brow furrowed, and swept her eyes back and forth as she considered the mystery. She paced along its length, stumped. Maybe the upper window was just for show? Nothing about these buildings made sense to her as it was; it wouldn’t surprise her if whole parts of them served no practical purpose . Frustrated and disappointed, she tilted her head back and sighed. Her eyes widened at the frayed string dangling from the ceiling. There was a rectangle cut in the material around it, as though it were an oddly-placed door. It was bordered by the same strips of wood that edged the meeting place of the walls and floor . Standing on the balls of her feet, she reached for the string. It was too high. She jumped, brushing the end and setting it to sway wildly. On her second attempt, she caught a solid hold of the string, using her weight to yank down . Dust showered her, and pain exploded in her temple as a ladder slid down from overhead and struck her . “Fuck!” Teeth clenched, she squeezed her eyes shut and pressed a hand over the throbbing point of impact. Tears welled in her eyes. She placed her other hand on the ladder to steady herself . “Should have a warning posted or something. Like a picture, making the danger clear — possible beheading when opened .” Lara opened her eyes and looked up at the opening in the ceiling. Motes of dust floated lazily through the air all around it. And, to her annoyance, she noticed a little sign on the side of the ladder. Black writing and images of little stick people — or were they bots? — getting hit by the ladder . “Yeah, that’s the perfect place for it .” The ache in her head continued as she climbed the ladder. Its old springs squeaked in protest of her weight . When her head emerged in the new space, she stopped. The air here was heavier and warmer than that in the hallway below. A thick layer of dust coated everything, all cast in the afternoon sunlight streaming through the rectangular window ahead. The ceiling was slanted, extremely low to either side and meeting in a high peak at the center . Lara climbed into the room, turning on hands and knees to look through the opening. The floor below was covered in clumps of dust. Without her weight on it, the ladder lifted slightly; she pressed down on it to keep it extended. Whatever
